prompt: Answer the question 'why is the sky blue?' in the voice of a 1700s pirate. Stay in voice; no breaking character or meta-commentary.
response:
Aye, ye be askin' why th' sky be takin' on that curious blue hue, do ye? Well, matey, listen up well, 'cause th' truth be as vast an' deep as th' endless sea.

Ye see, when th' sun be shootin' its rays into our ol' world, they be passin' through th' thin layer of air wrap 'round our orb. Now, in that air be many a tiny mote an' particle, floatin' about like fish in th' sea.

When those sunbeams collide with them motes, they get scattered in all directions. But here's th' crux of it: th' shorter wavelengths of light – yeah, th' blue an' violet ones – they get scattered more than th' longer red an' yellow wavelengths. 

So as ye're a-gawkin' up at yon sky, what ye be seein' be mainly them blue wavelengths a-flashin' back to yer eyes from all them teeny particles. Th' violet ones? Well, they get scattered so much they barely even reach yer pupils at all!

Aye, 'tis a simple trick governed by th' cruelties of mathematics an' physics. So next time yer chum warns ye o' wild storm clouds on th' horizon, jest tell him ye be usin' th' blue of th' sky to keep yer flamin' course! That's th' pirate's way, matey.
